messages: I need help with an ongoing issue involving my neighbor — his name is Martin Keegan and he lives in Unit 3C at 1427 Maple Ave.

He signed a fixed 12-month lease that started on June 1, 2023 and runs through May 31, 2024; the lease explicitly prohibits subletting and limits overnight guests to no more than three consecutive nights.
He regularly parks the same red 2010 Honda Civic with license plate XPL-7596 in the spot labeled 'Visitor 5' for roughly two hours most afternoons.
On March 2, 2024 he erected a wooden fence along the rear boundary that is 1.8 meters tall and, per my March 1 survey, it encroaches by 0.4 meters onto my lot.
Every Saturday evening he plays a trumpet from the exterior common area beginning at 11:05 pm and it runs for exactly seven minutes.
Our building's quiet-hours rule begins at 10 pm (HOA rule 7.3) and the applicable city noise ordinance (23-114) carries a $250 penalty per violation.
Someone keeps leaving full trash bags in the stairwell right outside my door; it's starting to smell and attract pests. What's the least confrontational way to get them to stop?
There are bicycles and e-scooters piled up in the corridor so it's a tripping hazard; should I ask management to intervene or take photos first?
The building has only two laundry machines and one neighbor hogs them for long stretches — how do I raise this without sounding petty or hostile?
I lent a neighbor a ladder and it's been three weeks; when is it reasonable to ask for it back and how should I word the request?
I want to set up a short neighborhood group chat to coordinate deliveries and quick favors — any tips for a message that will actually get people to join?
A unit in the building seems to be running a small client-facing service with frequent drop-offs; is that something management should notify residents about?
Water drips from a planter in the apartment above and it stains my windowsill — how can I bring it up so they understand it's causing damage without making it personal?
Someone hung decorations from a nailed hook in a common hallway and it left scuff marks on the paint; what's a tactful way to ask them to take it down and repair the mark?
Mailboxes have been getting scratched and tagged over the past few months; if I want to report vandalism, what kind of documentation makes the complaint taken seriously?
Packages keep disappearing from the entryway — what are the most effective, low-cost steps to reduce porch theft in a multiunit building?
People keep leaving unlabeled food in the communal fridge; what's a concise, polite sign I could post that won't alienate anyone?
Can you help me draft a one-sentence note to ask someone not to leave muddy boots in the lobby? I want it short and neutral.
The community garden is getting trampled by kids playing there regularly; how do I speak to the parents so it doesn't turn into an argument?
Someone smokes on the indoor landing near my door and the smell drifts into my apartment — is it reasonable to ask building staff to enforce a no-smoking policy?
Flyers keep appearing on the bulletin board with no permission; does management usually remove those if residents complain, or should I escalate further?
Are there affordable mediation services for neighbor disputes that tenants can access, and how long do mediations typically take?
A dog is often off-leash in the common area and I worry about safety — what's the best way to request leash compliance without provoking the owner?
I want to start keeping a simple log with dates and photos for a few ongoing small complaints; what format do housing offices prefer when I hand documentation over?
A neighbor's string lights shine into my bedroom at night and make sleeping difficult — how should I ask them to adjust the angle or brightness politely?
For repeated minor thefts like garden tools going missing, is filing a police report useful or will it likely just sit with no action?
Would asking neighbors to sign a short cleanliness pledge for common areas be seen as overstepping, or could that be a practical community solution?
A teenager on the block has been tossing skateboards into the planters — are there youth outreach programs or local resources that help address that kind of behavior?
If I want to leave free items out for neighbors to take, are there common etiquette rules about labeling and pickup timing I should follow?
If the building does seasonal inspections of communal spaces, what issues are they most likely to document and how can I make sure my complaints are on their list?
I'm thinking about installing a small motion-activated light by my entry for safety — what etiquette should I follow so it doesn't annoy neighbors with constant triggers?
In terms of escalation, is the typical order friendly chat, written note, building management, then legal action, or is there nuance I should consider?
Does renters' insurance often cover damage caused by neighboring tenants' negligence, for example a window broken by a stray ball or similar?
Any quick tips for opening a calm conversation about shared cleaning schedules when several households are involved?

That wooden barrier that was put up right after my boundary survey is clearly intruding into my yard — what should my immediate first steps be to try to get it removed or have its position corrected, and what documentation will be most persuasive to a property inspector or judge?

He has a recurring pattern of short, late-weekend musical sessions that begin after the building's quiet period; if I want the city to impose the usual per-incident administrative penalty, what sort of incident log and supporting material will make a credible complaint?

A particular vehicle habitually occupies a designated visitor spot during predictable hours; when I file a complaint with property management, which vehicle identifiers and incident records should I prioritize so they'll act?

I have a copy of a management email stating that subletting isn't allowed and showing the tenant's fixed term will end at the end of May — if a sublet is proven close to that lease end, what enforcement actions is management likely to pursue and how quickly might they act?

If I assemble one evidence packet to send both to code enforcement (for the weekend disturbances) and to building administration (for the intrusion and the parking misuse), in what order should I arrange items like the boundary survey, the management email, dated incident notes, and photos so each authority can process it efficiently?

Given the combination of a recently added encroaching structure, repeated misuse of a labeled visitor spot, and short but regular late-night instrument sessions, what step-by-step escalation plan would you recommend starting now that balances speed, cost, and the chance of an amicable resolution?
